food_subgroup: Mixed rice bowl with meat — braised pork and fried chicken cutlet over bibimbap-style rice with vegetables

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FOODON_00002572 covers bibimbap as a Korean mixed rice dish. This specific variant adds braised pork (lu rou) and fried chicken cutlet as protein toppings, which are not individually coded in FoodOn. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Bibimbap — Korean mixed rice with vegetables and meat
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers bibimbap as a composite Korean rice dish. The dual-protein variant (braised pork + chicken cutlet) is not separately coded; closest parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for bibimbap or this specific composite Korean rice bowl variant.

main_ingredients: mixed vegetables (spinach, bean sprouts, carrots, cucumber)
allergens: gluten (soy sauce, wheat coating on chicken cutlet)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising a bowl of steamed white rice topped with braised pork belly, a fried chicken cutlet, assorted seasoned vegetables, and gochujang sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Bibimbap (Korean mixed rice with meat and vegetables) — composite nutrient estimation based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for steamed white rice, braised pork belly, fried chicken cutlet, and mixed vegetables, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table values for Korean-style composite rice dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central SR Legacy) and FAO/INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact dual-protein bibimbap variant. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis: approximately 45% steamed white rice (USDA FDC #168878), 20% braised pork belly (USDA FDC #168289 adapted for braised preparation), 20% fried chicken cutlet (USDA FDC #171477 adapted for breaded fried preparation), and 15% mixed seasoned vegetables with gochujang and sesame oil. Values are cross-referenced against FAO/INFOODS SEA composite Korean rice dish benchmarks. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (boiled) and mixed rice dishes with protein and vegetables
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific braised pork and chicken cutlet bibimbap. GI is estimated at 54 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed Korean rice dishes with protein and vegetables, which consistently show GI reduction from plain white rice (GI ~72) to approximately 50–58 when combined with protein, fat, and fibre from meat and vegetable toppings. The addition of gochujang, sesame oil, and mixed vegetables further moderates the glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as (54 × 92.25 g carbohydrate per 450 g serving) / 100 = 49.9. gi_category set to low per validator threshold (GI ≤ 55 = low).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, gochujang, and braising liquid
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 380 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1,710 mg per 450 g serving), this dish provides a substantial portion of the 2,000 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-sodium preparation or limit portion size to half a bowl.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from white rice base, partially offset by protein and fat from braised pork and chicken cutlet
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated GI of 54 (low category) and GL of approximately 49.9 per serving indicate a moderate glycemic impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size, consider substituting brown rice, and pair this meal with a low-GI side to manage postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork belly and fried chicken cutlet, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat (approximately 1.8 g per 100 g) from braised pork belly and deep-fried chicken cutlet, together with high sodium content, warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing leaner protein preparations and requesting less sauce would reduce both saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Pork and Chicken Cutlet Bibimbap (卤肉鸡排拌饭; 브레이즈드 포크 치킨 커틀릿 비빔밥) is a fusion Korean-Chinese rice bowl dish served in Singapore, combining two distinct protein preparations over a base of steamed white rice in the bibimbap style. The dish features slow-braised pork belly (lu rou, 卤肉) — pork cooked in a soy-based master stock with five-spice, garlic, and sugar until tender and richly flavoured — alongside a crispy deep-fried chicken cutlet (鸡排) that is breaded and fried to a golden crust. These proteins are served over steamed white rice accompanied by assorted seasoned vegetables typical of bibimbap, such as blanched spinach, bean sprouts, julienned carrots, and cucumber, dressed with sesame oil and gochujang (Korean fermented chilli paste). The dish reflects the culinary fusion common in Singapore's Korean restaurant scene, where Chinese-style braised pork techniques are incorporated into the Korean bibimbap format. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 168 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein (9.2 g/100 g) from both pork and chicken, moderate carbohydrate from the rice base (20.5 g/100 g), and moderate fat (5.8 g/100 g). Sodium content is elevated at approximately 380 mg per 100 g due to soy sauce, gochujang, and braising liquid, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index of 54 (low category) reflects the moderating effect of protein, fat, and dietary fibre on the glycemic response of the white rice base. This dish is suitable for general consumption but requires moderation for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and saturated fat content.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact dual-protein bibimbap variant sold at Mincheng Bibimbap Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for the individual components (steamed white rice, braised pork belly, fried chicken cutlet, mixed vegetables), cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA benchmarks for Korean composite rice dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for mixed Korean rice dishes with protein and vegetables. Confidence is medium.
notes: 1. This dish is a fusion Korean-Chinese rice bowl served at Mincheng Bibimbap (민정비빔밥 敏丁拌) at Funan, Singapore (field survey data, food_id GD003902). 2. Nutrient values are estimated from component analysis; no exact approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries used for component estimation. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimation. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and water_g are estimated from component analysis and carry medium confidence. 6. The dish is not halal-certified as it contains pork. 7. GI estimated at 54 (low category, GI ≤ 55) based on University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for mixed Korean rice dishes with protein and vegetables. 8. GL per serving = (54 × 92.25) / 100 = 49.9, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 20.5 × 4.5 = 92.25 g. 9. The input food_name '卤肉鸡排饭 Braised Pork with Chicken Cutlet Bibimbap' has been normalised to canonical English 'Braised Pork and Chicken Cutlet Bibimbap'; the Chinese characters 卤肉鸡排饭 are preserved in aliases and local_names.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft constructed with component-based nutrient estimation, GI analogous estimate, ontology closest_parent assignments, Korean local name, Chinese simplified and traditional names, full health context, and LLM description.

Iteration 2: Addressed REJECT status from previous iteration. Identified that 'Korean (served in Singapore)' implies Singaporean multilingual requirements (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) in addition to Korean. Added Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names to `local_names` and corresponding entries to `aliases` and `aliases_by_language`. Verified aliases_by_language consistency. Verified GI 54 <= 55 correctly assigned gi_category 'low'. Verified -1 sentinel values for vitamin_d_mcg and selenium_mcg documented in notes. Verified ontology match_type values are all from permitted set. Verified zero-macro justification not required (no core macros are zero). Final self-score: 8/8, all hard gates pass. Decision: PASS.
